coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive luteolin electrochemical sensor based on zeolitic imidazolate frameworks-derived cobalt trioxide @ nitrogen doped carbon nanotube/amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots composites modified glass carbon electrode
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yang
Zhang, Li
Zhao, Pengcheng
Wang, Chenxi
Fei, Junjie
Xie, Yixi
coredata.dc:description: The accurate determination of luteolin is of great significance for food and pharmaceutical industry. Herein, an ultrasensitive electrochemical sensor for luteolin based on zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIF)-derived cobalt trioxide @ nitrogen doped carbon nanotube/amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots composites modified glass carbon electrode (Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE) was constructed. The Co3O4@N-CNTs composites were synthesized through direct pyrolysis of ZIF-67 in the N2 atmosphere, which exhibited outstanding electrochemical performance for luteolin due to the conductivity of N-CNTs and the catalytic action of Co3O4. Under optimized conditions, the Co3O4@N-CNTs /NH2-GQDs/GCE exhibited a linear range of 0.5–1000 nM for detecting luteolin, with a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.1 nM (S/N = 3). Furthermore, the sensor was successfully applied for the detection of luteolin in Chrysanthemum extracts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical sensors
ZIF-derived material
Co3O4@N-CNTs
NH2-GQD
Luteolin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical characterization of different modified electrodes
Effect of accumulation time and accumulation potential
Quantitative analysis of LU on Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE
Detection of LU in actual sample

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants Nos. 21874114 and 31701613). Xiangtan University Innovation Foundation for Postgraduate (XDCX2021B159).

The authors thank for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants Nos. 21874114 and 31701613 ). Xiangtan University Innovation Foundation for Postgraduate ( XDCX2021B159 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of different magnifications of ZIF-67 (A and B) and Co3O4@N-CNTs (C and D); TEM images of Co3O4@N-CNTs (E), NH2-GQD (F) and Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQD (G and H).

(A) EIS spectra of bare GCE (a), NH2-GQDs/GCE (b), Co3O4@N-CNTs/GCE (c) and Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQD/GCE (d); supporting electrolyte: 0.1 M KCl containing 1 mM Fe(CN)6 3−/4−; frequency range: 0.1 Hz to 100 KHz; (B) CVs of 1 μM LU on bare GCE (a), NH2-GQDs/GCE (b), Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQD/GCE (d) and Co3O4@N-CNTs/GCE (e) in 0.1 M PBS (pH = 5.5); c is the CV of the supporting electrolyte on Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQD/GCE.

(A) CVs of 1 μM LU on Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQD/GCE in various pH (a–h: 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 7.5, 8.0，8.5, 9.0) of supporting electrolyte solution. (B) The relationship between pH and oxidation peak and reduction peak potential; (C) CVs of Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQD/GCE in the presence of 1 μM LU at different scan rates (20–400 mv s−1); (D) The relationship between scan rates and oxidation peak and reduction peak current.

(A) DPVs obtained from Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQD/GCE in the presence of LU ranging from a to k (0.5, 5, 10, 20, 50, 80, 100, 300, 500, 800, 1000 nmol/L) in PBS; (B) The DPVs of linear calibration between the current responses and the concentration of LU; (C) Reproducibility study of seven individual electrodes in the same conditions; (D) Effect of some possible interfering substances on the determination of 1 μM LU in PBS solution.

DPV current response in the presence of different interference (A: QR, Mr, Mo; B: QR with different concentrations; C: Mo with different concentrations; D: Cy, Dio, EA; E: CGA, UA) in the LU solution on Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQD/GCE.
Schematic representation of the fabrication procedure of Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQD/GCE.
Comparison of the performance of Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQD/GCE with other reported LU sensors.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive luteolin electrochemical sensor based on zeolitic imidazolate frameworks-derived cobalt trioxide @ nitrogen doped carbon nanotube/amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots composites modified glass carbon electrode

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Huaihua University, Huaihua 418000, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: College of Chemistry, Xiangtan University, Xiangtan 411105, People’s Republic of China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The accurate determination of luteolin is of great significance for food and pharmaceutical industry. Herein, an ultrasensitive electrochemical sensor for luteolin based on zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIF)-derived cobalt trioxide @ nitrogen doped carbon nanotube/amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots composites modified glass carbon electrode (Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE) was constructed. The Co3O4@N-CNTs composites were synthesized through direct pyrolysis of ZIF-67 in the N2 atmosphere, which exhibited outstanding electrochemical performance for luteolin due to the conductivity of N-CNTs and the catalytic action of Co3O4. Under optimized conditions, the Co3O4@N-CNTs /NH2-GQDs/GCE exhibited a linear range of 0.5–1000 nM for detecting luteolin, with a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.1 nM (S/N = 3). Furthermore, the sensor was successfully applied for the detection of luteolin in Chrysanthemum extracts.

ce:section.ce:para: Luteolin (LU) is widely found in vegetables, medicinal herbs and fruits [1]. As an antioxidant, it exhibits versatile health benefit biological properties such as anti-inflammatory, antibacterial and anti-proliferative activities [2–5]. For its potential values in drugs and functional foods, it is meaningful to develop efficient methods for detecting LU.

Many analytical tools have been employed for LU detection, such as spectrophotometry [6], capillary electrophoresis [7], high-performance liquid chromatography [8], gas chromatography [9], and electrochemical sensor [10,11]. Among them, electrochemical sensor has attracted many interests because the characteristics of low cost, quick response, and convenient operation. However, most of the present LU electrochemical sensors are limited in actual use for the low current signal, narrow linear range and poor sensitivity [10,12,13]. Hence, it is significant to develop new LU electrochemical sensors with better performances.

For developing high performance LU electrochemical senor，many materials were employed for electrode surface modification, such as carbon materials (graphene, carbon nanotubes, carbon dots, etc.), metal and its oxide nanoparticles, polymer, biomaterials (DNA, antibody, enzyme, molecular beacon, etc.), and various composite materials [14–18]. Among them, carbon materials have attracted many interests due to the high conductivity, large specific surface area, abundant species, and relatively easy to obtain [19,20].

Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) are a kind of organic-inorganic hybrid materials with intra-molecular pores consist of metal ions or clusters and organic ligands mainly including nitrogen-containing heterocycles, polycarboxylic acids, and other mixed ligands [21]. MOFs have been reported as promising candidates for carbon-based materials [22,23]. By direct pyrolyzing the original MOF, MOF-derived carbon materials can be obtained [24]. Recently, carbon composite rich in carbon, nitrogen and catalytic active sites derived from zeolite imidazoline frameworks (ZIFs) which are a subclass of MOFs have attracted many interest [22,23,25,26]. For example, by carbonizing ZIF-67, carbon composites consisted of cobalt oxides and nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes could be obtained [27]. In addition to the excellent electroactivity from carbon nanotubes，the presence of cobalt oxides can further increase the active sites of the above carbon composites, which may make them a suitable candidate for electrode modified material.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are a kind of two-dimensional carbon nanomaterial having large specific surface area, and abundant active sites and accessible edges [28]. In addition, GQDs exhibited excellent dispersion and chemical stability, which was employed in constructing electrochemical sensors [29].

In this work, ZIF-derived cobalt trioxide @ nitrogen-doped carbon nanotubes (Co3O4@N-CNTs) with outstanding electrochemical activity and excellent conductivity were successfully synthesized by a one-step calcination method ( Scheme 1). Furthermore, Co3O4@N-CNTs /amino-functionalized GQDs composites (Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQD) were successfully prepared and applied to construct a novel electrochemical luteolin sensor. Benefiting from the good electrochemical activity of Co3O4@N-CNTs and NH2-GQDs, this sensor shows high sensitivity and good selectivity for LU detection. In addition, the proposed sensor has been successfully applied in detecting LU in actual chrysanthemum tea samples.

Cobalt nitrate hexahydrate（Co(NO3)2·6H2O) was from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). NH2-GQDs were obtained from XFNANO Materials Tech. Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China). Luteolin and 2-methylimidazole were obtained from Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Chlorogenic acid, ursolic acid, cynaroside (luteolin 7-glucoside), ellagic acid, and diosmetin were purchased from Chengdu Purechem-Standard Co., Ltd (Chengdu,China). The phosphate buffer solutions (PBS) were obtained by mixing sodium dihydrogen phosphate (0.1 M NaH2PO4) and sodium monohydrate phosphate (0.1 M Na2HPO4). All the solutions were prepared by deionized water.

The electrochemical measurements were recorded on a CHI-660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China). A platinum electrode was used as the counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl electrode was used as the reference electrode. The bare glassy carbon electrode and the modified electrodes were applied as working electrode. All the electrodes were obtained from Chenhua Co., Ltd. The characterizations of morphology and structure were performed by employing a Hitachi H7700 scanning electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan), a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Massachusetts, US), and a Philips X′pert Pro Super diffractometer. In addition, the specific surface area and pore size distribution of the materials were calculated based on the data from a 3H-2000 nitrogen adsorption BET specific surface tester of Beishide Instrument Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

Co3O4@N-CNTs were synthesized via a two-step route, including synthesis of ZIF-67 and pyrolysis. Firstly, ZIF-67 was prepared by the method reported previously with a little improvement [30]. Co(NO3)2·6H2O (1.455 g) and 2-Methylimidazole (1.642 g) were dissolved into 80.0 mL mixture of MeOH and EtOH in equal volume, respectively. Then, the solutions were mixed under stirring and further incubated at room temperature for 24 h. The resultant samples were firstly centrifuged, and then washed with MeOH and ultrapure water in sequence. After vacuum drying at 60 °C for 12 h, ZIF-67 was obtained. The ZIF-derived Co3O4@N-CNTs composites were prepared through a pyrolysis method that ZIF-67 powder was firstly put in a tube furnace and heated at 800 °C for 2 h in N2 atmosphere [31]. After cooling down to room temperature, the collected ZIF-derived Co3O4@N-CNTs composite were dispersed in N. N-dimethylformamide (1.0 mg/mL).

The bare GCEs were polished with 0.5 mm Al2O3 powder, and dried in air after ultrasonic cleaning in ethanol and ultrapure water for 5 min. Then, the Co3O4@N-CNTs suspension (1 mg mL−1) and NH2-GQDs dispersion (1 mg mL−1) were ultrasonically mixed at a ratio of 2:1 for 30 min 6 μL of Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs dispersion was dripped onto the above processed GCE, then dried under infrared lamp for future use. In addition, the comparative modified electrodes including bare GCE, NH2-GQD/GCE, and Co3O4@N-CNTs/GCE, were constructed by the same process.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves were recorded at a scan rate of 50 mV/s on the working electrode within a range of − 0.1 to 0.7 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed in a standard potassium ferricyanide solution system, of which the amplitude and pulse wide were respectively 50 mV and 5 mV. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was collected within 0–0.6 V for detecting LU. The electrode was electrochemically subjected to potential cycling between − 0.1 and 0.7 V in phosphate buffer (pH 5.5) at 50 mV s−1 until reaching a steady state before the next measurement.

The SEM diagrams of the obtained materials were shown in Fig. 1. As shown in Fig. 1(A and B), the particles were evenly distributed and presented as rhombic dodecahedral structures with diameters of 400–500 nm, indicating the successful synthesis of ZIF-67. The surface morphology and microstructure of Co3O4@N-CNTs were shown in Fig. 1(C and D). As can be seen, after the calcination at a high temperature for two hours, the shape of ZIF-67 became irregular and the surface became rough. CNTs grew on the surface and cobalt oxide particles are wrapped by CNTs. It was also observed that aggregation occurred between the particles due to weak magnetism of cobalt oxide and adsorption on rough surface. The microstructure of freestanding of Co3O4@N-CNTs and Co3O4@N-CNTs/ NH2-GQDs were investigated by TEM. It was shown in Fig. 1E, many carbon nanofibers were formed on the edge. In addition, different sizes of dark dots of cobalt nanoparticles were dispersed in the matrix. Then, NH2-GQDs were successfully coated with the solution of Co3O4@N-CNTs (Fig. 1G and H). NH2-GQDs, as uniformly dispersed nanomaterial, can better disperse Co3O4@N-CNTs.

The crystallographic structures of Co3O4@N-CNTs were further confirmed by XRD results. As shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting information)，there are three sharp diffraction peaks located at 44.2°, 50.8° and 75.82° which are attributed to (1 1 1), (2 0 0), and (2 2 0) crystal planes of cobalt, respectively. The broad peaks but sharp diffraction peak at 26.26° in the XRD belongs to graphite carbon, which is derived from carbon skeleton of ZIF-67. All these results suggested that Co3O4@N-CNTs possesses a better distribution of Co on the carbon matrix.

The elemental compositions and chemical state of Co3O4@N-CNTs were analyzed with the help of X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). As shown in Fig. S2, four elements including C, N, O and Co were presented，whose relative elemental contents were calculated to be 92.4%, 3.17%, 3.77%, and 0.66%, respectively. It is clear that the C elemental content was higher than previous reports [32]. The significantly increased C contents in the XPS spectrum of Co3O4@N-CNTs further confirms that as a precursor, ZIF-67 can form a large number of carbon nanotube structures by carbonizing at 800 °C for two hours without resorting to other carbon sources and relying on its own organic skeleton. The results indicated the successful preparation of the composite. Further observing the high resolution XPS spectrum of C1S (Fig. S2B), CC bond (284.1ev), CN bond (284.1ev), CN/CO (286.7ev) and OCO bond (290.0ev) were identified respectively. It was also revealed by the N 1s spectrum that pyridinic N (398.2 eV) and pyrrolic N(400.5 eV)were existed, as well as graphitic N (402.5 eV) and oxidized N (405.9 eV) (Fig. S2C). These results confirmed that N-CNTs were formed during the pyrolysis. It has been suggested that the pyridinic N of poor stability can form a hydrogen bond with hydroxyl of target compound, and consequently enhancing the electrocatalytic performance [33]. As seen in the Co 2p XPS spectrum, the peaks of 780.8 and 796.5 eV, 779.0 and 794.7 eV were assigned to Co2+ and Co3+, respectively (Fig. S2D). Benefiting from the more Co-Nx active sites were generated, the synthesized ZIF-derived material exhibited their potential electrocatalytic activity.

The pore distribution and specific surface area of the synthesized materials were investigated by nitrogen adsorption/desorption curves (Fig. S3). After pyrolysis, the specific surface area of ZIF-67 decreased (from 1811.49 to 210.77 m2/g), which was attributed to the structural collapse induced by the carbonization of the organic linker. In addition, the average pore size of the obtained Co3O4@N-CNTs composite concomitantly increased from 1.6 nm to 5.2 nm, indicating the formation of more mesoporous structures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of different modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the EIS consisted of a semicircular part and a linear part. The electron transfer impedance (Rct) which reflected the conductivity of composites is proportional to the radius of curvature of the Nyquist curve. Fig. 2A shows the EIS spectra of different modified electrodes. It can be clearly observed that the Co3O4@N-CNTs electrode possessed a low resistance, which was attributed to the excellent conductivity and electron transfer of Co3O4@N-CNTs. NH2-GQDs and bare GCE have the largest value of Rct, which indicated that poor conductivity and electron transfer. With poor conductivity, NH2-GQDs did not contribute to the conductivity and electron transfer of Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs, but probably promote the detection performance and stability of the modified electrode because of its excellent biocompatibility and dispersive property.

As shown in Fig. 2B, the electrochemical behavior of LU on different electrodes were characterized by CV with 1 μM LU in buffer solution (pH = 5.5). Among the tested electrodes, Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE exhibited the highest anodic peak current response (ipa(d) = −10.34 μA) for LU, while it did not show apparent response for the supporting electrode (curve c). Compared with the bare GCE (ipa = − 1.670 μA), Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE exhibited outstanding current signal, which indicated that the Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs shows excellent electrochemical properties. Comparing curve e and d, it was revealed that addition of NH2-GQDs to the composite apparently reduce the background current of Co3O4@N-CNTs and increase its response for LU. Being poorly conductive, NH2-GQDs had larger specific surface area and abundant surface active sites which increased the stability and film-forming property of the Co3O4@N-CNTs. Hence, it can be inferred that the addition of some materials of poor conductivity can improve the dispersion of the whole composites, and it will not have much effect on the conductivity of composites.

Compared with ZIF-67, ZIF-derived Co3O4@N-CNTs composite exhibited outstanding conductivity. After calcination at 800 °C for 2 h，ZIF-derived Co3O4@N-CNTs composite produces many carbon fiber structures, which accelerates electron transfer rate. In addition, the pyridinic N in ZIF-67, which can form a hydrogen bond with negatively charged of LU, promoted enrichment of LU on the Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs [32].

The difference in proportion of NH2-GQDs and Co3O4@N-CNTs has a prominent effect on the electrochemical activity of LU (Fig. S4A). With the increasing ratio of Co3O4@N-CNTs, the anodic peak current increases gradually. When two times Co3O4@N-CNTs added in Co3O4@N-CNTs, the response current reached relative maximum. Thus, as the ratio of Co3O4@N-CNTs and NH2-GQDs changes,

the peak current decreases to different degrees. The highest current signal for LU detection may be explained by the following mechanism: (1) Co3O4@N-CNTs contains nitrogen rich elements and carbon fiber structure with excellent electrical conductivity. (2) NH2-GQDs possess excellent biocompatibility and stabilizing ability. Adding a certain amount of NH2-GQDs can improve the stability of Co3O4@N-CNTs. When the content ratio of Co3O4@N-CNTs and NH2-GQDs is 2:1, the response current reached relative maximum and the detection performance was the best among the tested ratios. However, with the increase ratio of Co3O4@N-CNTs, the excellent electrochemical performance of Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs will become weaker. Thus, a proportion of 2:1 was chosen as the optimum ratio for fabricating Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs composite, which was selected as the electrode modifier in the following electrochemical experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of accumulation time and accumulation potential
ce:section.ce:para: To optimize the detection conditions, the effects of the accumulation process on the oxidation peak current and the related parameters were investigated by DPV method. As seen in Fig. S4C, in the range of − 0.2 to 0.3 V, the increasing accumulation potential firstly enhanced the peak current which reached the top at 0.1 V, then the peak current decreased until to the end of 0.3 V. Hence, 0.1 V was used as the best accumulation potential for the entire experiment. In addition, the maximum response peak current can be obtained, when the accumulation time up to 400 s. Due to the adsorption of LU by Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs composite has reached its maximum, the oxidation peak current decreased slightly (Fig. S4B). Thus, the suitable accumulation time was selected as 400 s

The pH influences the electron transfer of LU in the electrochemical process. The CV scans of LU under different pH on Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE were shown in Fig. 3A. As seen, with the increasing acidity of buffer solution, the oxidation and reduction peak potentials have shifted in the negative direction. This means that protons participate in the electrochemical reaction of LU, directly. The possible electrochemical reaction mechanism of LU on Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE was shown in Scheme S1. When the pH value was 5.5, the maximum value of the peak oxidation current was obtained. Therefore, the PBS with pH 5.5 was chosen as the solvent (Fig. S4D). When the pH value was less than 5.5, the peak oxidation current was increase gradually. However, the peak oxidation current showed a downward trend, when the pH value was more than 5.5. Moreover, the two peak potentials both have a linear relationship with pH. As shown in Fig. 3B, the linear equation is shown as follow: Epa (V) = − 0.058 pH + 0.6947 (R2 = 0.9961) and Epc (V) = − 0.051 pH + 0.5839 (R2 = 0.9951). The slope of the oxidation peak relationship is 58 mV/pH, which is almost identical to the theoretical value of 59 mV/pH. Hence, it is concluded that the number of electron gain and loss is the same as that of proton transfer in the redox process of LU on Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE.

The electrochemical responses of LU on Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE at different scan rate were evaluated by CV (Fig. 3C). The scan rate and the peak current presented a linear relationship within the scan rate increasing from 20 mV/s to 400 mV/s. As shown in Fig. 3D, the linear equation is shown as follow: Ipa (μA) = − 0.17256ν + 4.19423(R2 = 0.9987) and Ipc (μA) = 0.16451ν − 0.50345(R2 = 0.9974). The results suggested that the electrochemical behavior of LU on Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE is a process controlled by adsorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative analysis of LU on Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimum conditions, the quantitative analysis of LU with different concentrations on Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE was performed by DPV. Fig. 4A showed that the peak current exhibited obviously increase with the rising concentration of LU. At low LU concentrations (CLU), the Ipa values were proportional to the concentrations, similar results were also obtained at high concentrations. After plotting the Ipa value versus CLU, two linear relationships between Ipa and CLU were obtained in the range of low concentration and high concentration, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 4B, the linear equation was shown as follow: Ipa(μA) = − 0.07643 C(nM) - 3.31864 (0.5 nM ≤ C ≤ 100 nM R2 = 0.9901); Ipa(μA) = − 0.03502 C(nM) - 7.36947 (100 nM ≤ C ≤ 1000 nM R2 = 0.9976). According to results, the limit of detection (LOD) of the determination was calculated to be 0.1 nM (S/N = 3).

To evaluate the performances of the prepared sensor, the values of the linear range and LOD of the previous reported LU electrochemical sensors were listed in Table 1. As shown, the proposed sensor in this work had the lowest LOD value and very broad liner range. Considering the simple preparation process and the remarkable sensitivity, the present sensor had potential advantages in constructing LU detection platform. Besides, the prepared Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs composite also provided alternative materials for building other flavonoid sensors.

In practical analysis, many other compounds were existed, which might influence the accurate detection of LU. Hence, the anti-interference ability of the sensor was investigated. 50 times dosage of SO4 2−, K+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, and 10 times dosage of citric acid (CA), ascorbic acid (AA), oxalic acid (OA), L-cysteine(L-cys), and glucose(Glu) were successively added to PBS containing 0.1 μM LU and then the detection process was respectively performed. In addition, some natural flavonoids with o-dihydroxy moieties in B ring and phenolic acids may coexisted with LU in plants were also investigated as interference, including quercetin (QR), morin (Mo), myricetin (Mr), chlorogenic acid(CGA), ursolic acid (UA), cynaroside (luteolin 7-glucoside, Cy), ellagic acid (EA), and diosmetin (Dio). As shown in Fig. 4D, the addition of most compounds exposed no significant effects on the oxidation peak current of LU. In details, the variation oxidation peak currents of L-cysteine were less than 7%, and the rest of all the interfering substances change the currents were less than 4%. For flavonoid interfering components, their effects on the detection are varied. As shown in Fig. 5A, QR and Mr showed different peak potentials when detecting luteolin by DPV, and the peak current of luteolin varied very little when the concentrations of QR and Mr were less than LU. However, as the amounts of QR increased, its interference to LU detection enhanced (Fig. 5B). Similar results were also observed for Mo, but the signal of LU changed little with the variation of Mo amounts, indicating limited interferences (Fig. 5C). Fig. 5D revealed that the presence of Cy which was the glycosides of LU, apparently enhanced the response of LU. Besides, Dio with one hydroxyl group in its o-dihydroxy structure being replaced by methoxy group, showed no interference. In addition, phenolic acids such as CGA and UA showed no apparent interference, but EA showed obvious interference. All the results indicated that the sensor based on Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE constructed by us has good anti-interference ability for LU in systems without abundant flavonoid interfering components.

The stability of the sensor was studied in 0.1 M PBS butter solution. After 10 cycles of tested, the current response of Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE decreased by only 2.8%, which indicated that the detection of LU by Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE has good stability.

To evaluate the reproducibility of the sensors, seven separate Co3O4@N-CNTs/NH2-GQDs/GCE electrodes were prepared in the optimal conditions, and collected their current responses to LU (Fig. 4C). The sensors exhibited good reproducibility and the RSD of the current responses was 8%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of LU in actual sample
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the potentials of the prepared sensor in real sample analysis，LU in chrysanthemum extract was detected. Chrysanthemum was purchased from Xiangtan supermarket (Xiangtan, China). The sample was extracted by 60 mL of water and the solution was diluted 10 times with PBS (0.1 M, pH 5.5). Then the standard solutions of LU at different concentrations were added to the samples. The recovery rate was 96.7–106.3% (Tab.S1), which indicated that the proposed sensor could be used for the detection of LU in actual samples with outstanding sensitivity and reproducibility.

In this study, a type of nanomaterial (Co3O4@N-CNTs) was synthesized and used to construct an ultrasensitive electrochemical sensor to detect the LU. Benefiting from the outstanding electrochemical activity of Co3O4@N-CNTs and excellent biocompatibility, good dispersion effect and stability ability of NH2-GQDs, the prepared modified electrode exhibited ultrasensitive detection performance for LU with low LOD of 0.1 nM and broad detection range from 0.5 nM to1000 nM. In actual sample analysis, the senor also exhibited excellent sensitive, stability and repeatability. Hence, the present study may provide an alternative solution for ultrasensitive determination of LU and also promote the applications of MOFs derived carbon material in the sensing field.

Yang Hu: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Li Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Pengcheng Zhao: Investigation, Data curation. Chenxi Wang: Resources, Investigation, Validation. Junjie Fei: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Yixi Xie: Supervision, Formal analysis, Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants Nos. 21874114 and 31701613). Xiangtan University Innovation Foundation for Postgraduate (XDCX2021B159).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2021.130938.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yang Hu is currently pursuing his Sc.D. under the supervision of Prof. Yixi Xie at College of Chemistry, Xiangtan University, China. His research focuses on the construction of electrochemical sensors based on novel carbon nanomaterials.

Junjie Fei is currently a professor of College of Chemistry, Xiangtan University, China. He received his Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Wuhan University (China) in 2004. His work focuses on the design, construction and application of stimuli-responsive electrochemical sensors and novel high-sensitivity electrochemical sensors.

Yixi Xie is currently an associate professor of College of Chemistry, Xiangtan University, China. He received his Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Central South University (China) in 2014. His work focuses on the design, construction and application of new electrochemical sensors for polyphenol detection.
